text: Overloaded Operators and Friend Access C++ Interlude 5 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Overloaded Operators • To overload an operator, you define an operator method whose name has the form: operatorsymbol • where symbol is the operator that you want to overload • An overloaded operator has more than one meaning – In class instances, must define new meaning for operators such as the equality operator == – For lists they are equal if they have identical lengths and items © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Overloading == for Equality Linked list implementation of overloading == #include <iostream> struct Node { int data; Node* next; Node(int value) : data(value), next(nullptr) {} }; public: LinkedList() : head(nullptr) {} // Overloaded == operator to compare two linked lists bool operator==(const LinkedList& other) const { Node* current1 = head; Node* current2 = other.head; // Traverse both lists and compare the data of each node while (current1 && current2) { if (current1->data != current2->data) { return false; // Lists are not equal if any data doesn't match } current1 = current1->next; current2 = current2->next; } } // Both lists should end at the same time (same length) return current1 == nullptr && current2 == nullptr; } int main() { LinkedList list1; LinkedList list2; // Insert values into list1 list1.insert(1); list1.insert(2); // Insert values into list2 list2.insert(1); list2.insert(2); // Compare lists using overloaded == operator if (list1 == list2) { std::cout << "The lists are equal." << std::endl; } else { std::cout << "The lists are not equal." << std::endl; } return 0; } Overloading = for Assignment Linked list implementation of overloading = // Without overloaded = operator, you end up with shallow copy #include <iostream> struct Node { int data; Node* next; Node(int value) : data(value), next(nullptr) {} }; class LinkedList { private: Node* head; public: // Overloaded assignment operator to perform deep copy LinkedList& operator=(const LinkedList& other) { // Self-assignment check if (this == &other) { return *this; } // First, clear the current list this->clear(); // Deep copy: create a new list by copying nodes from the other list Node* temp = other.head; while (temp) { this->insert(temp->data); temp = temp->next; } return *this; } }; int main() { LinkedList list1; LinkedList list2; // Insert values into list1 list1.insert(1); list1.insert(2); /* Use the overloaded assignment operator to copy list1 into list2 */ list2 = list1; return 0; } int main() { LinkedList list1; LinkedList list2; // Insert values into list1 list1.insert(1); list1.insert(2); list1.insert(3); // Insert values into list2 list2.insert(4); list2.insert(5); list2.insert(6); /* Use the overloaded + operator to concatenate list1 and list2 */ LinkedList list3 = list1 + list2; // Display the result of concatenation std::cout << "Concatenated List (List 1 + List 2): "; list3.display(); return 0; } Overloading + for Concatenation #include <iostream> struct Node { int data; Node* next; Node(int value) : data(value), next(nullptr) {} }; class LinkedList { private: Node* head; public: // Overloaded + operator to concatenate two linked lists LinkedList operator+(const LinkedList& other) const { LinkedList result; Node* temp = head; // Insert all elements from the current list (this) while (temp) { result.insert(temp->data); temp = temp->next; } // Insert all elements from the other list temp = other.head; while (temp) { result.insert(temp->data); temp = temp->next; } return result; // Return the concatenated result list } }; Guidelines for Overloading Operators © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Overloading an operator is the same as writing a method whose name is operatorsymbol • where symbol is the operator that you want to overload. • Overloaded operators are typically placed in the public section of the class declaration. • When you decide to overload an operator, be sure that the new operation closely matches how the operator is already used. If that is not possible, consider creating a method whose name is not operatorsymbol to perform the operation. • You can overload any C++ operator except . .* :: ?: sizeof • Commonly overloaded operators are the assignment (=), equality (= = and !=), and relational (<, <=, >, >=) operators. • You cannot define new operators by overloading symbols that are not already C++ operators. • You cannot change the standard precedence of a C++ operator. • You cannot change the number of arguments for an overloaded operator, since they represent the operator’s operands. • At least one operand of an overloaded operator must be an instance of a class. End C++ Interlude 5 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Appendix C++ Interlude 5 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Overloaded Operators Linked list implementation of overloading == © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Overloading = for Assignment • Without overloaded assignment operator – End up with shallow copy • Implementation © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Friend Access and Overloading << • Functions and classes – Can be friends of a class • We wish to grant operator<< class access to private and protected data of LinkedList © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Friend Access and Overloading << A friend class © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. Overloaded Operators • To overload an operator, you define an operator method whose name has the form: operatorsymbol • where symbol is the operator that you want to overload • An overloaded operator has more than one meaning – In class instances, must define new meaning for operators such as the equality operator == – For lists they are equal if they have identical lengths and items © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Overloading == for Equality Linked list implementation of overloading == #include <iostream> struct Node { int data; Node* next; Node(int value) : data(value), next(nullptr) {} }; public: LinkedList() : head(nullptr) {} // Overloaded == operator to compare two linked lists bool operator==(const LinkedList& other) const { Node* current1 = head; Node* current2 = other.head; // Traverse both lists and compare the data of each node while (current1 && current2) { if (current1->data != current2->data) { return false; // Lists are not equal if any data doesn't match } current1 = current1->next; current2 = current2->next; } } // Both lists should end at the same time (same length) return current1 == nullptr && current2 == nullptr; } int main() { LinkedList list1; LinkedList list2; // Insert values into list1 list1.insert(1); list1.insert(2); // Insert values into list2 list2.insert(1); list2.insert(2); // Compare lists using overloaded == operator if (list1 == list2) { std::cout << "The lists are equal." << std::endl; } else { std::cout << "The lists are not equal." << std::endl; } return 0; }

Overloading = for Assignment Linked list implementation of overloading = // Without overloaded = operator, you end up with shallow copy #include <iostream> struct Node { int data; Node* next; Node(int value) : data(value), next(nullptr) {} }; class LinkedList { private: Node* head; public: // Overloaded assignment operator to perform deep copy LinkedList& operator=(const LinkedList& other) { // Self-assignment check if (this == &other) { return *this; } // First, clear the current list this->clear(); // Deep copy: create a new list by copying nodes from the other list Node* temp = other.head; while (temp) { this->insert(temp->data); temp = temp->next; } return *this; } }; int main() { LinkedList list1; LinkedList list2; // Insert values into list1 list1.insert(1); list1.insert(2); /* Use the overloaded assignment operator to copy list1 into list2 */ list2 = list1; return 0; }

int main() { LinkedList list1; LinkedList list2; // Insert values into list1 list1.insert(1); list1.insert(2); list1.insert(3); // Insert values into list2 list2.insert(4); list2.insert(5); list2.insert(6); /* Use the overloaded + operator to concatenate list1 and list2 */ LinkedList list3 = list1 + list2; // Display the result of concatenation std::cout << "Concatenated List (List 1 + List 2): "; list3.display(); return 0; } Overloading + for Concatenation #include <iostream> struct Node { int data; Node* next; Node(int value) : data(value), next(nullptr) {} }; class LinkedList { private: Node* head; public: // Overloaded + operator to concatenate two linked lists LinkedList operator+(const LinkedList& other) const { LinkedList result; Node* temp = head; // Insert all elements from the current list (this) while (temp) { result.insert(temp->data); temp = temp->next; } // Insert all elements from the other list temp = other.head; while (temp) { result.insert(temp->data); temp = temp->next; } return result; // Return the concatenated result list } };

Guidelines for Overloading Operators © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Overloading an operator is the same as writing a method whose name is operatorsymbol • where symbol is the operator that you want to overload. • Overloaded operators are typically placed in the public section of the class declaration. • When you decide to overload an operator, be sure that the new operation closely matches how the operator is already used. If that is not possible, consider creating a method whose name is not operatorsymbol to perform the operation. • You can overload any C++ operator except . .* :: ?: sizeof • Commonly overloaded operators are the assignment (=), equality (= = and !=), and relational (<, <=, >, >=) operators. • You cannot define new operators by overloading symbols that are not already C++ operators. • You cannot change the standard precedence of a C++ operator. • You cannot change the number of arguments for an overloaded operator, since they represent the operator’s operands. • At least one operand of an overloaded operator must be an instance of a class.
End C++ Interlude 5 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
